It might get confusing if a syntax error does not always match the actual source text:

```
julia> &#= not an infix operator :( =#3
ERROR: syntax: invalid syntax &3 around REPL[15]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[15]:1

julia> &           #=
       =#           3
ERROR: syntax: invalid syntax &3 around REPL[16]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[16]:1

julia> &               3
ERROR: syntax: invalid syntax &3 around REPL[17]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[17]:1
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53330","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53330/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53330/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53330/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53330","id":2134026171,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5_Mqe7","number":53330,"title":"Strange parser error message for `"x".3`","user":{"login":"llbit","id":2757386,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NTczODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2757386?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit","html_url":"https://github.com/llbit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-14T10:34:52Z","updated_at":"2024-02-14T10:34:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The expression `"x".3` gives the following error message in the REPL:
```
julia> "x".3
ERROR: cannot document the following expression:

0.3
```

This is strange compared to the error for `"x".y`:
```
julia> "x".y
ERROR: type String has no field y
```

Could this be updated to be more consistent between the two errors?

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920","id":2083476274,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM58L1My","number":52920,"title":"Redundant convert specialization for Number identity conversion","user":{"login":"llbit","id":2757386,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NTczODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2757386?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit","html_url":"https://github.com/llbit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-16T09:46:36Z","updated_at":"2024-01-16T09:46:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Given that the following function is defined at `Base.jl:96`:
```jl
convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where {T} = x
```
it seems that the specialization for `Number` at `number.jl:6` is redundant:
```jl
# Numbers are convertible
convert(::Type{T}, x::T)      where {T<:Number} = x
```
I asked on the slack helpdesk channel and got the answer that it could probably be removed.

There may be other similarly redundant identity-conversion functions. Checking `methods(convert)` I see that several appear to do identity conversion for different types like `AbstractChar` for example, but I've not checked those.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Checking `methods(convert)` I see that several appear to do identity conversion for different types like `AbstractChar` for example, but I've not checked those.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1893862198","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920#issuecomment-1893862198","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52920","id":1893862198,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5w4gs2","user":{"login":"llbit","id":2757386,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NTczODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2757386?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit","html_url":"https://github.com/llbit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-16T14:29:52Z","updated_at":"2024-01-16T14:29:52Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Ah, I see, that is interesting. I tried a small test program:
```jl
foo(::Type{T}, x::T) where T = 1
foo(::Type{T}, x::Number) where {T<:Number} = 2
foo(::Type{T}, x::T) where {T<:Number} = 3

println(foo(String, ""))
println(foo(Int, 1))
println(foo(UInt8, 0xFF))
```
In this case the third function is used for the last two calls but if I comment it out then the second function is used instead, exactly as you said. This dispatch behavior is a little bit counterintuitive to me but regardless it seems like the `Number` specialization needs to remain then to avoid an unnecessary call `T(x)`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1893862198/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

,"created_at":"2017-09-17T07:52:39Z","updated_at":"2024-01-16T15:05:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There are a number of situations in which Julia could choose to throw an ambiguity error, but instead establishes rules for prioritizing some types over others. In early "experimental" days the only real source for this information was the C code, but now that we're stabilizing it seems appropriate to document those rules. (Perhaps that documentation exists, but with a few-minute search I was unable to find it.)

Motivated by the question at https://discourse.julialang.org/t/method-dispatch-for-tuples/5925.

```jl
foo(::Type{T}, x::T) where T = 1
foo(::Type{T}, x::Number) where {T<:Number} = 2
```
A call to `foo(Int, 1)` returns `2`. My mental model of parameterized methods was that they were instantiated before dispatch/specificity but this seems to not be the case. I have not looked at the implementation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1893933298/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668","id":1364555822,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5RVXgu","number":46668,"title":"Struct field cannot have same name as type variable unless the type of the field is also the type variable","user":{"login":"llbit","id":2757386,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NTczODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2757386?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit","html_url":"https://github.com/llbit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-09-07T11:59:31Z","updated_at":"2022-09-07T11:59:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I noticed that a struct like `struct S{T} T::String end` does not compile. Therefore I expected that `struct S{T} T::T end` would likewise not compile because both of them have a field with the same name as a type variable (the error message seems to imply there is a name collision causing the problem). However, the latter case was accepted by the Julia compiler.

This seems like a compiler error or an oversight in Julia semantics. Also the error message could be greatly improved to explain what the compiler thinks is wrong in the context of the surface syntax.

```
julia> VERSION
v"1.8.0"

julia> struct S{T} T::String end
ERROR: syntax: function argument and static parameter names must be distinct around REPL[2]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> struct S{T} T::T end

julia>
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46668/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718","id":1370182678,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Rq1QW","number":46718,"title":"Certain assignment-form function declarations are not accepted when using type variables","user":{"login":"llbit","id":2757386,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NTczODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2757386?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit","html_url":"https://github.com/llbit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/llbit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-09-12T16:27:41Z","updated_at":"2022-09-12T16:27:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I expected `f(x::T)::Int64 where T = 1` to be equivalent to `function f(x::T)::Int64 where T 1 end`, yet the assignment form gives `UndefVarError: T not defined` and the non-assignment version is accepted without error.

The following forms do not cause an error despite using a type variable:

* `f(x::T) where T = 1` - just removing the declared return type fixes the error
* `f(x::Int64)::T where T = 1`  using `T` only in the return type is accepted
* `f(x)::T where T = 1` - removing the parameter type and using `T` as return type is also accepted

I tried several variations with multiple parameters and type variables and if at least one parameter had the type of a type variable and the return type was declared, it gave an error.
I do not understand why having a type variable as the type of a parameter should cause an error only if there is also a declared return type.

Tested in version `v"1.9.0-DEV.1301"`:

```
julia> VERSION
v"1.9.0-DEV.1301"

julia> f(x::T)::Int64 where T = 1
ERROR: UndefVarError: T not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> function f(x::T)::Int64 where T 1 end
f (generic function with 1 method)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}
